retrieved_docs.title: Understanding Delaware Prescribing and Distribution of Controlled Substances -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic pain correlates with several severe complications, including severe depression and suicide attempts and ideation. The lifetime prevalence for chronic pain patients attempting suicide attempt was shown to be between 5% and 14%; suicidal ideation was approximately 20% [13] These complications often require psychiatric intervention and advanced pharmacological or interventional therapies. Severe symptoms must receive treatment immediately, leading to an increase in healthcare costs. It is of the utmost importance to identify the risk factors and perform a thorough assessment of the patient with chronic pain as well as monitor for progression of symptoms. A team approach is an ideal way to limit the effects of chronic pain and its complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2406
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental poisoning by antidepressants is classified as a form of poisoning by psychotropic agents. Antidepressants, which are psychotropic medications used to treat mood disorders, can cause toxic effects when ingested unintentionally or in overdose, leading to an accidental poisoning scenario. Recognizing that such incidents fall under the broader category of psychotropic agent poisoning helps clinicians assess risk, implement appropriate treatment protocols, and develop preventive strategies for medication safety.

Overall, the taxonomy integrates medication‑, biologic‑, chemical‑, and illicit‑substance poisonings, their mental‑health sequelae, and associated behavioral contexts (e.g., suicide, drug abuse) into a unified framework that supports accurate diagnosis, coding, and public‑health surveillance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2259
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by analgesic drug is identified as a specific form of drug poisoning (التسمم بالعقاقير), placing it within the broader category of toxic exposures to medications. This type of poisoning is linked to suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, highlighting its relevance as a method of self‑harm in clinical and forensic contexts. Additionally, poisoning by analgesic drug is classified under the umbrella of poisoning by ketamine, indicating that it shares a hierarchical relationship with other drug‑specific poisonings and is considered a subcategory within the spectrum of ketamine‑related toxic events. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing analgesic drug poisoning both as a distinct clinical entity and as part of larger patterns of intentional self‑injury and drug‑related toxicology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2266
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs is classified as a late effect of poisoning due to a drug, medicinal, or biological substance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by analgesic drug is identified as a specific form of drug poisoning that falls under the broader Arabic category التسمم بالعقاقير (“poisoning by drugs”). This type of poisoning is linked to suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, highlighting its importance in assessments of self‑harm and mental‑health‑related risk. Similarly, Poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics is also classified as a subcategory of التسمم بالعقاقير, indicating that it shares the same overarching classification of drug‑related toxic exposures. Both analgesic‑drug poisoning and ketamine poisoning represent distinct subtypes within the general spectrum of drug poisoning, rather than a direct hierarchical relationship, and together illustrate the variety of toxic agents that can be involved in intentional or accidental overdose scenarios. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in diagnosing, documenting, and managing poisoning cases, especially when evaluating potential self‑injurious behavior.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 637
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by drugs and biologics is organized as a broad clinical category that includes several more specific sub‑categories. Within this hierarchy, poisoning by vaccine and/or biological substances and poisoning by water and/or mineral‑metabolism drugs are identified as child entities of the overall “POISONING BY DRUGS AND BIOLOGICALS” group. The same parent group also relates to “Poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics,” which in turn is placed under the Arabic‑language category “التسمم بالعقاقير” (poisoning by drugs), indicating that “التسمم بالعقاقير” serves as a higher‑level classification for various drug‑related poisonings. “Poisoning by analgesic drug” is a more narrowly defined entity that is linked to intentional self‑harm; it has a documented relationship with suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, highlighting its relevance in mental‑health and forensic contexts. This specific analgesic poisoning is also described as a subtype of “Poisoning by ketamine,” suggesting that it falls within a broader ketamine‑related poisoning framework. At the same time, “Poisoning by analgesic drug” is connected to the overarching “التسمم بالعقاقير” category, reinforcing that it is part of the general drug‑poisoning spectrum. Overall, the knowledge structure depicts a hierarchical taxonomy in which “التسمم بالعقاقير” (poisoning by drugs) encompasses broad groups such as “POISONING BY DRUGS AND BIOLOGICALS” and more focused entities like “Poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics.” Within these layers, specific poisonings—such as those caused by analgesic drugs—are linked to both broader toxicological categories (e.g., ketamine poisoning) and clinical outcomes (e.g., suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury). This integrated framework aids clinicians and public‑health professionals in classifying, diagnosing, and managing drug‑related toxic exposures across different levels of specificity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1819
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental poisoning by antidepressants is classified as a form of poisoning by psychotropic agents. The medication fenelzin is also linked to poisoning by psychotropic agents, indicating that exposure to fenelzin can result in similar toxic effects. Both concepts underscore the risk of toxic reactions associated with psychotropic substances and highlight the importance of careful handling and prevention of accidental ingestion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2256
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad category of medical conditions that arise as a result of toxic exposure, and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances is recognized as a specific type within this category. This relationship indicates that any disorder classified under “Disorder due to and following poisoning” can include cases where the toxic agent is a non‑medicinal substance, helping clinicians to organize and address poisoning‑related health issues more precisely.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1297
retrieved_docs.content: Suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury are classified as forms of poisoning (التسمم). Poisoning (التسمم) is associated with drug‑induced toxicity from medications indicated for lupus (تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين) and also linked to adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية). Recognizing that self‑inflicted injuries may be categorized under poisoning and that poisoning can arise from specific drug exposures or adrenal disorders aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 298
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses both التسمم (poisoning) and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances as specific subtypes. Within this framework, التسمم is also classified under the category of suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, highlighting that poisoning is a recognized method of self‑harm. Moreover, التسمم is associated with تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين (a form of drug‑induced poisoning) and with متلازمة الأدرينالية (adrenal syndrome), indicating that poisoning can co‑occur with or contribute to these particular clinical conditions. This integrated view underscores the interrelationship between general poisoning disorders, specific toxic exposures, self‑inflicted injury, and related systemic syndromes, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 484
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by analgesic drug, poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics, poisoning by other medications and drugs, poisoning caused by biological substance, and poisoning due to chemical substance are all presented as subcategories of a broader drug‑related poisoning concept, denoted in Arabic as “تسمم بالهيروين” (heroin poisoning) and, for some entries, as “التسمم بالعقاقير” (poisoning by drugs). This hierarchical organization reflects that, despite differing etiologies—analgesic agents, combined analgesic/antipyretic/antirheumatic agents, other medicinal products, biological agents, and chemical agents—each specific poisoning is considered a type of drug‑related toxic exposure. Poisoning by analgesic drug is additionally linked to intentional self‑harm, being associated with suicide and other intentional self‑inflicted injuries, underscoring its relevance in mental‑health and emergency contexts. The same entity is also described as a subtype of “Poisoning by ketamine,” indicating that analgesic drug poisoning can be viewed within the spectrum of ketamine‑related toxicities. Poisoning by other medications and drugs is connected to the concept of “late effect of poisoning due to drug, medicinal, or biological substance,” highlighting that adverse outcomes may persist long after the initial exposure. Finally, tryptophan poisoning is identified as a specific form of both “Poisoning caused by biological substance” and “Poisoning due to chemical substance,” illustrating that certain toxins can be classified simultaneously under biological and chemical poisoning categories. Together, these relationships portray a comprehensive framework in which diverse toxic exposures are organized under overarching drug‑poisoning categories, while also acknowledging distinct clinical contexts such as self‑inflicted injury, specific agent hierarchies, and delayed health effects.
